questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Mass number, atomic number and isotopes.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how a positive ion forms.

questions.stem: An atom becomes a positive ion. What has happened to the atom?
questions.question: An atom becomes a positive ion. What has happened to the atom?
options: It has gained one or more electrons.
It has lost one or more electrons.
It has gained one or more neutrons.
It has lost one or more protons from the nucleus.
questions.correctAnswer: It has lost one or more electrons.
questions.explanation: A positive ion forms when an atom loses electrons. The nucleus does not lose protons during ion formation.

distractorRationales.text: It has gained one or more electrons.
distractorRationales.misconception: positive ion confused with negative ion

distractorRationales.text: It has gained one or more neutrons.
distractorRationales.misconception: ion formation confused with isotope formation

distractorRationales.text: It has lost one or more protons from the nucleus.
distractorRationales.misconception: ion formation confused with nuclear change

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how a positive ion forms.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Mass number, atomic number and isotopes.

learningObjective.statement: Define isotopes using protons and neutrons.

questions.stem: Which statement describes isotopes of the same element?
questions.question: Which statement describes isotopes of the same element?
options: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
They have the same number of neutrons but different numbers of protons.
They have different numbers of protons and electrons.
They have no electrons in their outer shells.
questions.correctAnswer: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
questions.explanation: Atoms of the same element have the same number of protons. Isotopes differ because they have different numbers of neutrons.

distractorRationales.text: They have the same number of neutrons but different numbers of protons.
distractorRationales.misconception: element identity confused with neutron number

distractorRationales.text: They have different numbers of protons and electrons.
distractorRationales.misconception: isotope confused with ion

distractorRationales.text: They have no electrons in their outer shells.
distractorRationales.misconception: isotope confused with electron arrangement

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Define isotopes using protons and neutrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Mass number, atomic number and isotopes.

learningObjective.statement: Use atomic number and mass number to find particle numbers.

questions.stem: A neutral atom of carbon-14 has atomic number 6. How many protons, neutrons and electrons does it contain?
questions.question: A neutral atom of carbon-14 has atomic number 6. How many protons, neutrons and electrons does it contain?
options: 6 protons, 8 neutrons and 6 electrons.
6 protons, 14 neutrons and 6 electrons.
8 protons, 6 neutrons and 8 electrons.
14 protons, 6 neutrons and 14 electrons.
questions.correctAnswer: 6 protons, 8 neutrons and 6 electrons.
questions.explanation: Atomic number gives protons, so carbon has 6 protons. Neutrons = 14 - 6 = 8. A neutral atom has the same number of electrons as protons.

distractorRationales.text: 6 protons, 14 neutrons and 6 electrons.
distractorRationales.misconception: mass number used as neutron number

distractorRationales.text: 8 protons, 6 neutrons and 8 electrons.
distractorRationales.misconception: proton and neutron numbers swapped

distractorRationales.text: 14 protons, 6 neutrons and 14 electrons.
distractorRationales.misconception: mass number used as proton number

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Use atomic number and mass number to find particle numbers.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Mass number, atomic number and isotopes.

learningObjective.statement: Compare isotopes of the same element.

questions.stem: Magnesium-24 and magnesium-26 are isotopes. What is different in their nuclei?
questions.question: Magnesium-24 and magnesium-26 are isotopes. What is different in their nuclei?
options: The number of protons.
The number of neutrons.
The number of electrons in a neutral atom.
The charge on each proton.
questions.correctAnswer: The number of neutrons.
questions.explanation: Isotopes of magnesium have the same proton number but different mass numbers, so the number of neutrons is different.

distractorRationales.text: The number of protons.
The number of electrons in a neutral atom.
The charge on each proton.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Compare isotopes of the same element.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Mass number, atomic number and isotopes.

learningObjective.statement: Relate proton number and electron number in a neutral atom.

questions.stem: A neutral atom has 17 protons. How many electrons does it have?
questions.question: A neutral atom has 17 protons. How many electrons does it have?
questions.explanation: A neutral atom has equal numbers of protons and electrons, so it has 17 electrons.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Relate proton number and electron number in a neutral atom.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Mass number, atomic number and isotopes.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the particle that defines an element.

questions.stem: Two atoms are atoms of the same element. Which particle number must be the same in both atoms?
questions.question: Two atoms are atoms of the same element. Which particle number must be the same in both atoms?
options: Number of protons.
Number of neutrons.
Mass number.
Number of occupied electron shells.
questions.correctAnswer: Number of protons.
questions.explanation: The number of protons defines the element. Isotopes can have different numbers of neutrons.

distractorRationales.text: Number of neutrons.
Mass number.
Number of occupied electron shells.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the particle that defines an element.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Mass number, atomic number and isotopes.

learningObjective.statement: Distinguish ions from isotopes.

questions.stem: Which change would make a neutral atom into an ion but not change it into a different isotope?
questions.question: Which change would make a neutral atom into an ion but not change it into a different isotope?
options: Changing the number of electrons.
Changing the number of neutrons.
Changing the number of protons.
Changing the mass of a proton.
questions.correctAnswer: Changing the number of electrons.
questions.explanation: Ions form when electrons are gained or lost. Changing neutron number makes a different isotope; changing proton number makes a different element.

distractorRationales.text: Changing the number of neutrons.
Changing the number of protons.
Changing the mass of a proton.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Distinguish ions from isotopes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use balanced nuclear equations for alpha and beta decay.

learningObjective.statement: Predict the nuclear change caused by alpha decay.

questions.stem: A nucleus emits an alpha particle. What happens to its mass number and atomic number?
questions.question: A nucleus emits an alpha particle. What happens to its mass number and atomic number?
options: Mass number decreases by 4 and atomic number decreases by 2.
Mass number decreases by 2 and atomic number decreases by 4.
Mass number stays the same and atomic number increases by 1.
Mass number and atomic number both stay the same.
questions.correctAnswer: Mass number decreases by 4 and atomic number decreases by 2.
questions.explanation: An alpha particle contains 2 protons and 2 neutrons, so mass number decreases by 4 and atomic number decreases by 2.

distractorRationales.text: Mass number decreases by 2 and atomic number decreases by 4.
distractorRationales.misconception: alpha mass and charge changes swapped

distractorRationales.text: Mass number stays the same and atomic number increases by 1.
distractorRationales.misconception: alpha confused with beta-minus

distractorRationales.text: Mass number and atomic number both stay the same.
distractorRationales.misconception: alpha confused with gamma

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Predict the nuclear change caused by alpha decay.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use balanced nuclear equations for alpha and beta decay.

learningObjective.statement: Predict the nuclear change caused by beta-minus decay.

questions.stem: A nucleus emits beta-minus radiation. What happens to the atomic number of the nucleus?
questions.question: A nucleus emits beta-minus radiation. What happens to the atomic number of the nucleus?
options: It decreases by 2.
It decreases by 1.
It stays the same.
It increases by 1.
questions.correctAnswer: It increases by 1.
questions.explanation: In beta-minus decay a neutron changes into a proton, so the atomic number increases by 1 while mass number stays the same.

distractorRationales.text: It decreases by 2.
It decreases by 1.
It stays the same.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Predict the nuclear change caused by beta-minus decay.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use balanced nuclear equations for alpha and beta decay.

learningObjective.statement: State the effect of gamma emission on a nucleus.

questions.stem: A nucleus emits gamma radiation only. What happens to its mass number and atomic number?
questions.question: A nucleus emits gamma radiation only. What happens to its mass number and atomic number?
options: Both decrease.
Only mass number decreases.
Only atomic number increases.
Neither changes.
questions.correctAnswer: Neither changes.
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation is electromagnetic radiation from the nucleus. It does not change mass number or atomic number.

distractorRationales.text: Both decrease.
Only mass number decreases.
Only atomic number increases.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: State the effect of gamma emission on a nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use balanced nuclear equations for alpha and beta decay.

learningObjective.statement: Balance nuclear equations using mass number and atomic number.

questions.stem: In a balanced nuclear equation, which quantities must balance on both sides?
questions.question: In a balanced nuclear equation, which quantities must balance on both sides?
options: Mass number and atomic number.
Only mass number.
Only the chemical symbols.
Temperature and pressure.
questions.correctAnswer: Mass number and atomic number.
questions.explanation: Balanced nuclear equations conserve total mass number and total atomic number.

distractorRationales.text: Only mass number.
Only the chemical symbols.
Temperature and pressure.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Balance nuclear equations using mass number and atomic number.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use balanced nuclear equations for alpha and beta decay.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate a daughter mass number after alpha decay.

questions.stem: Uranium-238 emits an alpha particle. What is the mass number of the daughter nucleus?
questions.question: Uranium-238 emits an alpha particle. What is the mass number of the daughter nucleus?
questions.explanation: Alpha emission reduces mass number by 4, so 238 - 4 = 234.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate a daughter mass number after alpha decay.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use balanced nuclear equations for alpha and beta decay.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate a daughter atomic number after beta-minus decay.

questions.stem: Carbon-14 has atomic number 6. It emits beta-minus radiation. What is the atomic number of the daughter nucleus?
questions.question: Carbon-14 has atomic number 6. It emits beta-minus radiation. What is the atomic number of the daughter nucleus?
questions.explanation: Beta-minus emission increases atomic number by 1, so the daughter nucleus has atomic number 7.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate a daughter atomic number after beta-minus decay.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Describe radioactive decay as random.

questions.stem: Which statement about radioactive decay is correct?
questions.question: Which statement about radioactive decay is correct?
options: The exact nucleus that decays next can be predicted.
The exact time a particular unstable nucleus will decay is random.
Heating a radioactive source stops it decaying.
All nuclei in a sample decay at the same time.
questions.correctAnswer: The exact time a particular unstable nucleus will decay is random.
questions.explanation: Radioactive decay is random. It is not possible to predict exactly when a particular nucleus will decay.

distractorRationales.text: The exact nucleus that decays next can be predicted.
Heating a radioactive source stops it decaying.
All nuclei in a sample decay at the same time.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Describe radioactive decay as random.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Compare the penetration of alpha, beta and gamma radiation.

questions.stem: Which row gives the correct order of penetrating power, from least to greatest?
questions.question: Which row gives the correct order of penetrating power, from least to greatest?
options: alpha, beta, gamma
alpha, gamma, beta
gamma, beta, alpha
beta, alpha, gamma
questions.correctAnswer: alpha, beta, gamma
questions.explanation: Alpha is the least penetrating, beta is more penetrating, and gamma is the most penetrating.

distractorRationales.text: alpha, gamma, beta
gamma, beta, alpha
beta, alpha, gamma

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Compare the penetration of alpha, beta and gamma radiation.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Compare ionising power of nuclear radiation.

questions.stem: Which type of nuclear radiation is the most strongly ionising?
questions.question: Which type of nuclear radiation is the most strongly ionising?
options: Alpha
Beta
Gamma
Infrared
questions.correctAnswer: Alpha
questions.explanation: Alpha radiation is the most strongly ionising of alpha, beta and gamma radiation.

distractorRationales.text: Beta
Gamma
Infrared

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Compare ionising power of nuclear radiation.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Recall the meaning of activity.

questions.stem: What is activity a measure of?
questions.question: What is activity a measure of?
options: The number of decays per second.
The speed of alpha particles.
The distance gamma rays travel.
The temperature of the source.
questions.correctAnswer: The number of decays per second.
questions.explanation: Activity is the rate at which unstable nuclei decay. It is measured in becquerels, where 1 Bq is 1 decay per second.

distractorRationales.text: The speed of alpha particles.
The distance gamma rays travel.
The temperature of the source.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the meaning of activity.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate count rate from detector data.

questions.stem: A detector records 180 counts in 60 seconds. What is the count rate?
questions.question: A detector records 180 counts in 60 seconds. What is the count rate?
options: 0.33 counts per second
3 counts per second
120 counts per second
240 counts per second
questions.correctAnswer: 3 counts per second
questions.explanation: Count rate = total counts divided by time, so 180 / 60 = 3 counts per second.

distractorRationales.text: 0.33 counts per second
120 counts per second
240 counts per second

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate count rate from detector data.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Correct count rate for background radiation.

questions.stem: A source gives a measured count rate of 42 counts per second. The background count rate is 8 counts per second. What is the corrected count rate for the source?
questions.question: A source gives a measured count rate of 42 counts per second. The background count rate is 8 counts per second. What is the corrected count rate for the source?
options: 8 counts per second
34 counts per second
42 counts per second
50 counts per second
questions.correctAnswer: 34 counts per second
questions.explanation: Subtract background count rate from the measured count rate: 42 - 8 = 34 counts per second.

distractorRationales.text: 8 counts per second
42 counts per second
50 counts per second

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.2.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Correct count rate for background radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Uses of nuclear radiation and suitable source choices.

learningObjective.statement: Distinguish contamination from irradiation.

questions.stem: What does radioactive contamination mean?
questions.question: What does radioactive contamination mean?
options: A person has been exposed to radiation from a nearby source.
Radioactive material is on or inside an object or person.
A material has absorbed visible light.
A source has stopped emitting radiation.
questions.correctAnswer: Radioactive material is on or inside an object or person.
questions.explanation: Contamination means radioactive material is present on or inside something. Irradiation means exposure to radiation from a source.

distractorRationales.text: A person has been exposed to radiation from a nearby source.
A material has absorbed visible light.
A source has stopped emitting radiation.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.3.3.
questions.senNote: Checks: Distinguish contamination from irradiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Uses of nuclear radiation and suitable source choices.

learningObjective.statement: Choose a suitable half-life for a radioactive tracer.

questions.stem: A radioactive tracer is used inside the body. Which half-life is usually most suitable?
questions.question: A radioactive tracer is used inside the body. Which half-life is usually most suitable?
options: Long enough to take measurements but short enough to limit dose.
As long as possible, so it stays radioactive for years.
As short as possible, so no measurement can be taken.
Half-life does not matter for a tracer.
questions.correctAnswer: Long enough to take measurements but short enough to limit dose.
questions.explanation: A medical tracer needs to remain active long enough for measurements, but not so long that it gives unnecessary dose.

distractorRationales.text: As long as possible, so it stays radioactive for years.
As short as possible, so no measurement can be taken.
Half-life does not matter for a tracer.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.3.3.
questions.senNote: Checks: Choose a suitable half-life for a radioactive tracer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Uses of nuclear radiation and suitable source choices.

learningObjective.statement: Explain the use of alpha radiation in smoke alarms.

questions.stem: Why is alpha radiation suitable for use in some smoke alarms?
questions.question: Why is alpha radiation suitable for use in some smoke alarms?
options: It is weakly ionising and passes through thick lead.
It strongly ionises air but is stopped by smoke particles.
It has no charge and no mass.
It turns smoke into radioactive material.
questions.correctAnswer: It strongly ionises air but is stopped by smoke particles.
questions.explanation: Alpha radiation ionises air in the detector. Smoke reduces the ionisation reaching the detector, triggering the alarm.

distractorRationales.text: It is weakly ionising and passes through thick lead.
It has no charge and no mass.
It turns smoke into radioactive material.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.3.3.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain the use of alpha radiation in smoke alarms.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Uses of nuclear radiation and suitable source choices.

learningObjective.statement: Choose safety precautions for ionising radiation.

questions.stem: Which action reduces the dose received from an ionising radiation source?
questions.question: Which action reduces the dose received from an ionising radiation source?
options: Standing closer to the source.
Increasing exposure time.
Using shielding between the person and the source.
Removing all warning labels.
questions.correctAnswer: Using shielding between the person and the source.
questions.explanation: Dose can be reduced by increasing distance, reducing exposure time and using suitable shielding.

distractorRationales.text: Standing closer to the source.
Increasing exposure time.
Removing all warning labels.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.3.3.
questions.senNote: Checks: Choose safety precautions for ionising radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Uses of nuclear radiation and suitable source choices.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why gamma radiation can sterilise medical equipment.

questions.stem: Medical equipment can be sterilised using gamma radiation. Why is gamma radiation useful for this?
questions.question: Medical equipment can be sterilised using gamma radiation. Why is gamma radiation useful for this?
options: It is highly penetrating and can kill microorganisms.
It makes the equipment permanently radioactive.
It is stopped by a thin sheet of paper.
It removes all atoms from the equipment.
questions.correctAnswer: It is highly penetrating and can kill microorganisms.
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation is penetrating and can kill microorganisms. Irradiating equipment does not make it radioactive.

distractorRationales.text: It makes the equipment permanently radioactive.
It is stopped by a thin sheet of paper.
It removes all atoms from the equipment.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.3.3.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why gamma radiation can sterilise medical equipment.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Uses of nuclear radiation and suitable source choices.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why ionising radiation can be hazardous.

questions.stem: Why can ionising radiation increase the risk of cancer?
questions.question: Why can ionising radiation increase the risk of cancer?
options: It can damage DNA in cells.
It cools cells to absolute zero.
It removes all neutrons from cells.
It always makes a person radioactive.
questions.correctAnswer: It can damage DNA in cells.
questions.explanation: Ionising radiation can damage DNA. If cell division is affected, this can increase the risk of cancer.

distractorRationales.text: It cools cells to absolute zero.
It removes all neutrons from cells.
It always makes a person radioactive.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.4.3.3.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why ionising radiation can be hazardous.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: State current as the rate of flow of charge.

questions.stem: Which statement describes electric current in a metal wire?
questions.question: Which statement describes electric current in a metal wire?
options: The rate of flow of electric charge.
The energy transferred by each coulomb of charge.
The opposition to the flow of charge.
The amount of charge stored in the battery.
questions.correctAnswer: The rate of flow of electric charge.
questions.explanation: A larger current means more charge passes the same point each second. The unit of current is the ampere.

distractorRationales.text: The energy transferred by each coulomb of charge.
distractorRationales.misconception: potential difference confused with current

distractorRationales.text: The opposition to the flow of charge.
distractorRationales.misconception: resistance confused with current

distractorRationales.text: The amount of charge stored in the battery.
distractorRationales.misconception: charge store confused with current

options: ampere
coulomb
volt
ohm

questions.explanation: Electric charge is measured in coulombs. Current is measured in amperes.

volt

learningObjective.statement: Calculate charge using current and time.

questions.stem: A current of 3 A flows for 20 s. How much charge passes a point in the circuit?
questions.question: A current of 3 A flows for 20 s. How much charge passes a point in the circuit?
questions.explanation: Charge = current x time, so Q = 3 x 20 = 60 C.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.1.2.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate current from charge and time.

questions.stem: A charge of 48 C passes a point in 12 s. What is the current?
questions.question: A charge of 48 C passes a point in 12 s. What is the current?
options: 4 A
questions.correctAnswer: 4 A
questions.explanation: Current = charge / time, so I = 48 / 12 = 4 A.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate current from charge and time.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate time using charge and current.

questions.stem: A current of 0.5 A transfers 30 C of charge. For how long did the current flow?
questions.question: A current of 0.5 A transfers 30 C of charge. For how long did the current flow?
questions.explanation: Time = charge / current, so t = 30 / 0.5 = 60 s.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.1.2.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Describe how to connect an ammeter.

questions.stem: How should an ammeter be connected to measure the current through a lamp?
questions.question: How should an ammeter be connected to measure the current through a lamp?
options: In series with the lamp.
In parallel with the lamp.
Across the battery only.
Outside the complete circuit.
questions.correctAnswer: In series with the lamp.
questions.explanation: An ammeter must be connected in series so the same current passes through the meter and the lamp.

distractorRationales.text: In parallel with the lamp.

distractorRationales.text: Across the battery only.
distractorRationales.misconception: supply measurement confused with component current

distractorRationales.text: Outside the complete circuit.
distractorRationales.misconception: meter not connected in conducting path

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Link potential difference to current in a fixed resistor.

questions.stem: A cell is added in series with another identical cell. What happens to the current in a simple circuit with a fixed resistor?
questions.question: A cell is added in series with another identical cell. What happens to the current in a simple circuit with a fixed resistor?
options: It decreases because there are more cells.
It increases because the potential difference is larger.
It becomes zero because the cells share charge.
It stays the same because resistance is unchanged.
questions.correctAnswer: It increases because the potential difference is larger.
questions.explanation: Adding cells in series increases the potential difference. For a fixed resistance, a larger potential difference gives a larger current.

distractorRationales.text: It decreases because there are more cells.
It becomes zero because the cells share charge.
It stays the same because resistance is unchanged.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.1.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Link potential difference to current in a fixed resistor.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why current needs a complete circuit.

questions.stem: A switch is open in a series circuit. What is the current in the circuit?
questions.question: A switch is open in a series circuit. What is the current in the circuit?
options: Zero.
The same as before.
Half the previous value.
Larger than before.
questions.correctAnswer: Zero.
questions.explanation: An open switch breaks the circuit, so there is no complete conducting path and the current is zero.

distractorRationales.text: The same as before.
Half the previous value.
Larger than before.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.1.2.

questions.question: Two lamps are connected in series. The current through the first lamp is 0.20 A. What is the current through the second lamp?

questions.explanation: The current is the same at every point in a series circuit.

questions.question: What carries charge through a metal wire?

Protons moving through the wire.
Neutrons moving between atoms.
Positive ions leaving the battery.

questions.explanation: In a metal, electric charge is carried by electrons that can move through the lattice.

learningObjective.statement: Relate amperes to coulombs per second.

questions.stem: A charge of 1 C passes a point in a wire each second. What is the current?
questions.question: A charge of 1 C passes a point in a wire each second. What is the current?
options: 1 A
2 A

options: High voltage reduces current for the same power, reducing heating losses.

High voltage makes cable resistance disappear.
High voltage changes the supply from AC to DC.
questions.correctAnswer: High voltage reduces current for the same power, reducing heating losses.
questions.explanation: The key reason is efficiency: high transmission voltage gives lower current for the same power, so less energy is wasted heating the cables.

distractorRationales.text: High voltage removes the need for power stations.
distractorRationales.misconception: grid purpose misunderstood

distractorRationales.text: High voltage makes cable resistance disappear.
distractorRationales.misconception: resistance elimination misconception

distractorRationales.text: High voltage changes the supply from AC to DC.
distractorRationales.misconception: ac/dc misconception

questions.stem: A transmission line transfers 8.0 MW at 400 kV. What is the current in the line?
questions.question: A transmission line transfers 8.0 MW at 400 kV. What is the current in the line?
questions.explanation: Use P = VI. Current = 8.0 x 10^6 ÷ 4.0 x 10^5 = 20 A.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Compare transmission current when potential difference changes by a scale factor.

questions.stem: The same 2.0 MW is transmitted at 20 kV and at 200 kV. Which statement about the current is correct?
questions.question: The same 2.0 MW is transmitted at 20 kV and at 200 kV. Which statement about the current is correct?
options: The current at 200 kV is one tenth of the current at 20 kV.
The current at 200 kV is ten times the current at 20 kV.
The current is the same because the power is the same.
The current at 20 kV is zero because the voltage is lower.
questions.correctAnswer: The current at 200 kV is one tenth of the current at 20 kV.
questions.explanation: For the same power, current is inversely proportional to potential difference. Increasing the voltage by a factor of 10 reduces the current by a factor of 10.

distractorRationales.text: The current at 200 kV is ten times the current at 20 kV.
distractorRationales.misconception: inverse relationship reversed

distractorRationales.text: The current is the same because the power is the same.
distractorRationales.misconception: voltage ignored in P=VI

distractorRationales.text: The current at 20 kV is zero because the voltage is lower.
distractorRationales.misconception: lower voltage mistaken for no current

A resistor only works at 0 degrees Celsius.

questions.question: Two identical lamps are connected in series to a battery. What happens to the current as it passes through the first lamp?
options: The current is used up by the first lamp.
The current becomes larger after the first lamp.
The current stays the same through both lamps.
The current changes into potential difference.

distractorRationales.text: The current is used up by the first lamp.
The current becomes larger after the first lamp.
The current changes into potential difference.

questions.question: A second identical cell is added in series with the first cell in a simple lamp circuit. What is the most likely effect?

learningObjective.statement: Explain a fault caused by a low-resistance bypass in a circuit.

questions.stem: A wire is accidentally connected directly across a lamp in a series circuit. Why might the lamp go out?
questions.question: A wire is accidentally connected directly across a lamp in a series circuit. Why might the lamp go out?
options: Most current takes the low-resistance path through the wire.
The wire changes current into charge.
The lamp has gained extra potential difference.
The battery has stopped producing charge.
questions.correctAnswer: Most current takes the low-resistance path through the wire.
questions.explanation: A connecting wire has very low resistance, so it can provide an easier path that bypasses the lamp.

distractorRationales.text: The wire changes current into charge.
The lamp has gained extra potential difference.
The battery has stopped producing charge.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.2.

questions.question: One lamp fails in a string of lamps connected in series. Why do the other lamps also go out?
options: The failed lamp breaks the only path for current.
The failed lamp takes all the potential difference but current still flows.
The other lamps have become parallel branches.
The current has been stored in the failed lamp.
questions.correctAnswer: The failed lamp breaks the only path for current.
questions.explanation: If one component in a series circuit breaks the loop, there is no complete path for current through the remaining components.

distractorRationales.text: The failed lamp takes all the potential difference but current still flows.
The other lamps have become parallel branches.
The current has been stored in the failed lamp.

learningObjective.statement: Compare potential differences across unequal series resistors.

questions.stem: A 2 ohm resistor and a 4 ohm resistor are connected in series. Which resistor has the larger potential difference across it?
questions.question: A 2 ohm resistor and a 4 ohm resistor are connected in series. Which resistor has the larger potential difference across it?
options: The 2 ohm resistor, because it has less resistance.
The 4 ohm resistor, because the same current flows through a larger resistance.
They must both have 0 V because they are in series.
They must always have equal potential differences.
questions.correctAnswer: The 4 ohm resistor, because the same current flows through a larger resistance.
questions.explanation: The same current flows through both series resistors. Since V = IR, the larger resistance has the larger potential difference.

distractorRationales.text: The 2 ohm resistor, because it has less resistance.
They must both have 0 V because they are in series.
They must always have equal potential differences.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate power using current and resistance.

questions.stem: A current of 3.0 A flows through a 4.0 ohm resistor. What power is transferred by the resistor?
questions.question: A current of 3.0 A flows through a 4.0 ohm resistor. What power is transferred by the resistor?
questions.explanation: Use P = I² × R. P = 3.0² × 4.0 = 36 W.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.4.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate power using current and resistance.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Compare power when current changes at the same potential difference.

questions.stem: Two lamps are connected to the same potential difference. Lamp X has twice the current of lamp Y. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: Two lamps are connected to the same potential difference. Lamp X has twice the current of lamp Y. Which statement is correct?
options: Lamp X transfers energy at twice the rate of lamp Y.
Lamp Y transfers energy at twice the rate of lamp X.
Both lamps must have the same power.
Lamp X transfers no energy because its current is larger.
questions.correctAnswer: Lamp X transfers energy at twice the rate of lamp Y.
questions.explanation: For the same potential difference, P = V × I, so doubling the current doubles the power.

distractorRationales.text: Lamp Y transfers energy at twice the rate of lamp X.
distractorRationales.misconception: power-current relationship reversed

distractorRationales.text: Both lamps must have the same power.
distractorRationales.misconception: current difference ignored

distractorRationales.text: Lamp X transfers no energy because its current is larger.
distractorRationales.misconception: larger current misunderstood

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.4.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Compare power when current changes at the same potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate energy transferred using power and time.

questions.stem: A 60 W lamp is switched on for 120 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.question: A 60 W lamp is switched on for 120 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.explanation: Use energy transferred = power × time. E = 60 × 120 = 7200 J.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.4.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate energy transferred using power and time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Energy transfers in everyday electrical appliances.

learningObjective.statement: Describe energy transfers in a domestic electrical appliance.

questions.stem: A hair dryer is connected to the mains supply. Which useful energy transfer is it designed to make?
questions.question: A hair dryer is connected to the mains supply. Which useful energy transfer is it designed to make?
options: Electrical energy transfer to thermal and kinetic energy stores.
Thermal energy transfer to electrical energy stores.
Nuclear energy transfer to kinetic energy stores.
Gravitational energy transfer to chemical energy stores.
questions.correctAnswer: Electrical energy transfer to thermal and kinetic energy stores.
questions.explanation: A hair dryer uses electrical energy transfer to heat air and move it with a fan.

distractorRationales.text: Thermal energy transfer to electrical energy stores.
distractorRationales.misconception: energy transfer direction reversed

distractorRationales.text: Nuclear energy transfer to kinetic energy stores.
distractorRationales.misconception: unrelated energy store

distractorRationales.text: Gravitational energy transfer to chemical energy stores.
distractorRationales.misconception: wrong store and pathway

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Energy transfers in everyday electrical appliances.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate energy transferred using power and time.

questions.stem: A 900 W microwave is used for 120 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.question: A 900 W microwave is used for 120 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.explanation: Use energy transferred = power × time. E = 900 × 120 = 108 000 J.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.4.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate energy transferred using power and time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Energy transfers in everyday electrical appliances.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate energy transferred using charge and potential difference.

questions.stem: A charge of 40 C passes through a motor with a potential difference of 6.0 V across it. How much energy is transferred?
questions.question: A charge of 40 C passes through a motor with a potential difference of 6.0 V across it. How much energy is transferred?
questions.explanation: Use energy transferred = charge × potential difference. E = 40 × 6.0 = 240 J.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.2.4.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate energy transferred using charge and potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Energy transfers in everyday electrical appliances.

learningObjective.statement: Compare energy transferred when power is constant and time changes.

questions.stem: Two identical heaters have the same power. Heater X is switched on for twice as long as heater Y. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: Two identical heaters have the same power. Heater X is switched on for twice as long as heater Y. Which statement is correct?
options: Heater X transfers twice as much energy as heater Y.
Heater Y transfers twice as much energy as heater X.
Both heaters transfer the same energy whatever the time.
Neither heater transfers energy because power is constant.
questions.correctAnswer: Heater X transfers twice as much energy as heater Y.
questions.explanation: For the same power, energy transferred is proportional to time. Twice the time means twice the energy transferred.

distractorRationales.text: Heater Y transfers twice as much energy as heater X.
distractorRationales.misconception: time relationship reversed

distractorRationales.text: Both heaters transfer the same energy whatever the time.
distractorRationales.misconception: time ignored

distractorRationales.text: Neither heater transfers energy because power is constant.
distractorRationales.misconception: constant power misunderstood

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare energy transferred when power is constant and time changes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Energy transfers in everyday electrical appliances.

learningObjective.statement: Relate appliance power rating to energy transferred over the same time.

questions.stem: A 2.0 kW kettle and a 1.0 kW heater are each used for 5 minutes. Which appliance transfers more energy?
questions.question: A 2.0 kW kettle and a 1.0 kW heater are each used for 5 minutes. Which appliance transfers more energy?
options: The kettle, because it has the larger power rating.
The heater, because smaller power always means more energy.
They transfer the same energy because they are used for the same time.
Neither transfers energy unless charge is stored inside it.
questions.correctAnswer: The kettle, because it has the larger power rating.
questions.explanation: For the same time, the appliance with the larger power rating transfers more energy.

distractorRationales.text: The heater, because smaller power always means more energy.
distractorRationales.misconception: power-energy relationship reversed

distractorRationales.text: They transfer the same energy because they are used for the same time.
distractorRationales.misconception: power difference ignored

distractorRationales.text: Neither transfers energy unless charge is stored inside it.
distractorRationales.misconception: energy transfer and charge storage confused

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Recall the efficiency equation.

questions.stem: Which equation can be used to calculate the efficiency of a device?
questions.question: Which equation can be used to calculate the efficiency of a device?
options: efficiency = useful output energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer
efficiency = total input energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
efficiency = wasted energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
efficiency = force ÷ distance
questions.correctAnswer: efficiency = useful output energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer
questions.explanation: Efficiency is the useful output energy transfer divided by the total input energy transfer. It can be multiplied by 100 to give a percentage.

distractorRationales.text: efficiency = total input energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
distractorRationales.misconception: ratio inverted

distractorRationales.text: efficiency = wasted energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
distractorRationales.misconception: wasted transfer used as numerator

distractorRationales.text: efficiency = force ÷ distance
distractorRationales.misconception: unrelated equation selected

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the efficiency equation.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate percentage efficiency from useful and total energy.

questions.stem: A motor transfers 80 J of energy usefully from 200 J supplied. What is its efficiency?
questions.question: A motor transfers 80 J of energy usefully from 200 J supplied. What is its efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input = 80 ÷ 200 = 0.40, which is 40%.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate percentage efficiency from useful and total energy.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Convert decimal efficiency to percentage efficiency.

questions.stem: A device has an efficiency of 0.65. What is this efficiency as a percentage?
questions.question: A device has an efficiency of 0.65. What is this efficiency as a percentage?
questions.explanation: To convert a decimal efficiency to a percentage, multiply by 100. 0.65 = 65%.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Convert decimal efficiency to percentage efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate wasted energy from total and useful energy transfers.

questions.stem: A lamp receives 500 J of electrical energy and transfers 75 J as useful light. How much energy is wasted?
questions.question: A lamp receives 500 J of electrical energy and transfers 75 J as useful light. How much energy is wasted?
questions.explanation: Wasted energy = total input energy - useful output energy = 500 - 75 = 425 J.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate wasted energy from total and useful energy transfers.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate useful output energy from percentage efficiency.

questions.stem: A machine is 30% efficient. It receives 600 J of energy. How much energy is transferred usefully?
questions.question: A machine is 30% efficient. It receives 600 J of energy. How much energy is transferred usefully?
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate useful output energy from percentage efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate input energy from useful output and efficiency.

questions.stem: A pump is 50% efficient and transfers 300 J usefully. How much energy was supplied to the pump?
questions.question: A pump is 50% efficient and transfers 300 J usefully. How much energy was supplied to the pump?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input, so total input = 300 ÷ 0.50 = 600 J.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate input energy from useful output and efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Explain why efficiency cannot exceed 100%.

questions.stem: Why can the efficiency of a normal energy transfer device not be greater than 100%?
questions.question: Why can the efficiency of a normal energy transfer device not be greater than 100%?
options: The useful output energy cannot be greater than the total input energy.
All devices destroy their input energy.
Useful output energy is always zero.
Efficiency is measured in joules, so it has no upper limit.
questions.correctAnswer: The useful output energy cannot be greater than the total input energy.
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved. A device cannot transfer more useful energy out than the total energy supplied to it.

distractorRationales.text: All devices destroy their input energy.
Useful output energy is always zero.
Efficiency is measured in joules, so it has no upper limit.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why efficiency cannot exceed 100%.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Interpret arrow width in a Sankey diagram.

questions.stem: In a Sankey diagram, what does the width of an arrow show?
questions.question: In a Sankey diagram, what does the width of an arrow show?
options: The amount of energy transferred.
The speed of the energy transfer.
The temperature of the device.
The voltage across the device.
questions.correctAnswer: The amount of energy transferred.
questions.explanation: In Sankey diagrams, arrow width represents the amount of energy transferred along that pathway.

distractorRationales.text: The speed of the energy transfer.
The temperature of the device.
The voltage across the device.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret arrow width in a Sankey diagram.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Calculate efficiency from values shown on a Sankey diagram.

questions.stem: A Sankey diagram shows 100 J input, 70 J useful output and 30 J wasted output. What is the efficiency?
questions.question: A Sankey diagram shows 100 J input, 70 J useful output and 30 J wasted output. What is the efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input = 70 ÷ 100 = 0.70 = 70%.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate efficiency from values shown on a Sankey diagram.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Compare efficiencies of two devices.

questions.stem: Device X transfers 40 J usefully from 100 J supplied. Device Y transfers 90 J usefully from 150 J supplied. Which device is more efficient?
questions.question: Device X transfers 40 J usefully from 100 J supplied. Device Y transfers 90 J usefully from 150 J supplied. Which device is more efficient?
options: Device Y
Device X
Both have the same efficiency.
Neither transfers energy usefully.
questions.correctAnswer: Device Y
questions.explanation: Device X is 40% efficient. Device Y is 90 ÷ 150 = 60% efficient, so Device Y is more efficient.

distractorRationales.text: Device X
Both have the same efficiency.
Neither transfers energy usefully.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Compare efficiencies of two devices.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Explain how reducing wasted transfers can improve efficiency.

questions.stem: Which change would usually increase the efficiency of a machine?
questions.question: Which change would usually increase the efficiency of a machine?
options: Reducing wasted energy transfers to the surroundings.
Increasing unwanted heating in the machine.
Making the useful output smaller while keeping the input the same.
Adding friction to moving parts.
questions.correctAnswer: Reducing wasted energy transfers to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Efficiency increases when a larger fraction of the input energy is transferred usefully, often by reducing wasted transfers.

distractorRationales.text: Increasing unwanted heating in the machine.
Making the useful output smaller while keeping the input the same.
Adding friction to moving parts.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how reducing wasted transfers can improve efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Explain how lubrication can improve efficiency.

questions.stem: Why can lubricating the moving parts of a machine improve its efficiency?
questions.question: Why can lubricating the moving parts of a machine improve its efficiency?
options: It reduces friction, so less energy is wasted by heating.
It creates extra energy in the machine.
It makes the input energy zero.
It changes wasted sound into voltage.
questions.correctAnswer: It reduces friction, so less energy is wasted by heating.
questions.explanation: Lubrication reduces friction. Less energy is transferred by unwanted heating, so a greater fraction can be useful.

distractorRationales.text: It creates extra energy in the machine.
It makes the input energy zero.
It changes wasted sound into voltage.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how lubrication can improve efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Explain how insulation reduces wasted energy transfer.

questions.stem: A hot-water tank is insulated. How does this improve efficiency?
questions.question: A hot-water tank is insulated. How does this improve efficiency?
options: Less energy is wasted by heating the surroundings.
More energy is destroyed inside the tank.
The tank no longer needs an energy input.
The water stops having a thermal store.
questions.correctAnswer: Less energy is wasted by heating the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Insulation reduces unwanted energy transfer to the surroundings, so more of the supplied energy remains useful for heating the water.

distractorRationales.text: More energy is destroyed inside the tank.
The tank no longer needs an energy input.
The water stops having a thermal store.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how insulation reduces wasted energy transfer.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Recognise that efficiency is a ratio without a unit.

questions.stem: Which statement about efficiency is correct?
questions.question: Which statement about efficiency is correct?
options: Efficiency has no unit because it is a ratio.
Efficiency is measured in joules.
Efficiency is measured in watts.
Efficiency is measured in metres per second.
questions.correctAnswer: Efficiency has no unit because it is a ratio.
questions.explanation: Efficiency is a ratio of two energy transfers, so the units cancel. It may be given as a decimal or percentage.

distractorRationales.text: Efficiency is measured in joules.
Efficiency is measured in watts.
Efficiency is measured in metres per second.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Recognise that efficiency is a ratio without a unit.

learningObjective.statement: Find wasted percentage from efficiency percentage.

questions.stem: A device is 75% efficient. What percentage of its input energy is wasted?
questions.question: A device is 75% efficient. What percentage of its input energy is wasted?
questions.explanation: If 75% is useful, the remaining 25% is wasted because the total input is 100%.
questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Find wasted percentage from efficiency percentage.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Compare useful and wasted transfers using Sankey arrow widths.

questions.stem: A Sankey diagram for a motor has a useful output arrow wider than the wasted output arrow. What does this show?
questions.question: A Sankey diagram for a motor has a useful output arrow wider than the wasted output arrow. What does this show?
options: More energy is transferred usefully than is wasted.
The motor is exactly 100% efficient.
The wasted transfer is larger than the useful transfer.
The motor has no input energy.
questions.correctAnswer: More energy is transferred usefully than is wasted.
questions.explanation: A wider arrow represents a larger energy transfer, so the useful transfer is larger than the wasted transfer.

distractorRationales.text: The motor is exactly 100% efficient.
The wasted transfer is larger than the useful transfer.
The motor has no input energy.

learningObjective.statement: Identify useful output transfers for efficiency calculations.

questions.stem: A kettle is designed to heat water. Which output transfer is useful?
questions.question: A kettle is designed to heat water. Which output transfer is useful?
options: Energy transferred to the thermal store of the water.
Energy transferred by sound to the room.
Energy transferred to heat the air around the kettle.
Energy transferred to warm the handle.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy transferred to the thermal store of the water.
questions.explanation: The useful output of a kettle is the energy transferred to the thermal store of the water.

distractorRationales.text: Energy transferred by sound to the room.
Energy transferred to heat the air around the kettle.
Energy transferred to warm the handle.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.2.2.
questions.senNote: Checks: Identify useful output transfers for efficiency calculations.

questions.question: A fan is 35% efficient and a pump is 0.42 efficient. Which device has the higher efficiency?
options: The pump
The fan
They have the same efficiency.
It cannot be compared because one value is a decimal.

questions.explanation: 0.42 is 42%, which is higher than 35%, so the pump has the higher efficiency.

distractorRationales.text: The fan
They have the same efficiency.

learningObjective.statement: Identify named energy stores.

questions.stem: Which row gives only energy stores?
questions.question: Which row gives only energy stores?
options: kinetic, thermal, chemical
heating, radiation, electrical work
force, current, voltage
conduction, convection, evaporation
questions.correctAnswer: kinetic, thermal, chemical
questions.explanation: Kinetic, thermal and chemical are energy stores. Heating, radiation and electrical work are pathways by which energy is transferred.

distractorRationales.text: heating, radiation, electrical work

distractorRationales.text: force, current, voltage
distractorRationales.misconception: physical quantities confused with stores

distractorRationales.text: conduction, convection, evaporation
distractorRationales.misconception: processes confused with stores

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: State conservation of energy in a closed system.

questions.stem: In a closed system, what happens to the total energy?
questions.question: In a closed system, what happens to the total energy?
options: It stays the same.
It always increases.
It is destroyed when objects slow down.
It becomes only useful energy.
questions.correctAnswer: It stays the same.
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved in a closed system. It can be transferred between stores, but the total amount stays the same.

distractorRationales.text: It always increases.
It is destroyed when objects slow down.
distractorRationales.misconception: dissipation confused with destruction

distractorRationales.text: It becomes only useful energy.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.1.1.
questions.senNote: Checks: State conservation of energy in a closed system.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Describe energy transfers for a falling object.

questions.stem: A ball falls freely from rest. Ignoring air resistance, which energy transfer happens as it falls?
questions.question: A ball falls freely from rest. Ignoring air resistance, which energy transfer happens as it falls?
options: From gravitational potential store to kinetic store.
From kinetic store to chemical store.
From thermal store to elastic potential store.
From nuclear store to magnetic store.
questions.correctAnswer: From gravitational potential store to kinetic store.
questions.explanation: As the ball falls, its height decreases and its speed increases, so energy transfers from the gravitational potential store to the kinetic store.

distractorRationales.text: From kinetic store to chemical store.
From thermal store to elastic potential store.
From nuclear store to magnetic store.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.1.1.

questions.subject: Physics

learningObjective.statement: Explain why a bouncing object reaches a lower height.

questions.stem: A ball bounces and reaches a lower height after each bounce. What has happened to some of its energy?
questions.question: A ball bounces and reaches a lower height after each bounce. What has happened to some of its energy?
options: It has been transferred to the thermal stores of the ball and surroundings.
It has been destroyed during the impact.
It has changed into mass.
It has become gravitational potential energy in the ground.
questions.correctAnswer: It has been transferred to the thermal stores of the ball and surroundings.
questions.explanation: Some energy is dissipated by heating and sound during each bounce, so less energy remains in the ball’s mechanical stores.

distractorRationales.text: It has been destroyed during the impact.
distractorRationales.misconception: energy destruction misconception

distractorRationales.text: It has changed into mass.
It has become gravitational potential energy in the ground.

questions.authorNotes: Authored exam-style item for AQA 4.1.1.1.

gravitational potential store of the car on a level road

nuclear store of the brakes
gravitational potential store of the car on a level road

questions.question: A lamp is designed to produce visible light but it also becomes warm. Which transfer is wasted?

Energy transferred electrically to the lamp.

Energy is destroyed and no longer exists.
Energy is stored only in a battery.
Energy changes into force.

questions.question: A vacuum flask has a vacuum between its walls. Which transfer is mainly reduced by the vacuum?
options: Conduction and convection through the space between the walls.
Radiation through the space only.
Electrical transfer through the drink.
Chemical transfer from the flask to the drink.
questions.correctAnswer: Conduction and convection through the space between the walls.

Electrical transfer through the drink.
Chemical transfer from the flask to the drink.

It makes the glass a better conductor.
It converts thermal energy into chemical energy.
